skill.name: openalex-database
skill.description: Query OpenAlex scholarly records, analyze research output, find citations, and export bibliometric results with Python helpers.
skill.summary: Search and analyze scholarly literature through the OpenAlex API.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found the command and weak-crypto alerts are Markdown or keyword false positives, while the real behaviors are intended OpenAlex API requests and CSV export examples. The skill is publishable with a warning because it contains executable Python scripts that make network requests and may generate large API workloads.

medium_findings.title: Network Requests to Public OpenAlex API
medium_findings.description: The Python client sends user queries and an optional email address to the public OpenAlex API. This is the core function of the skill, but users should know that search terms and mailto values leave the local environment. No evidence found of credential exfiltration or unexpected third-party endpoints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The requests.get call and OpenAlex base URL are direct evidence of outbound network behavior. The context is legitimate scholarly API access, and I found no suspicious external destination.

medium_findings.title: Large Queries Can Produce Heavy API Usage
medium_findings.description: Pagination and sampling helpers can make repeated API calls for large result sets. The client includes rate limiting and retry logic, but broad queries may consume time, quota, and network bandwidth.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pagination loop and large-sample branch clearly perform repeated requests. The risk is operational, not malicious, because the code targets OpenAlex and includes limiting controls.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Writes Are Documentation Export Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis found file write patterns in CSV export examples. These examples write user-requested research results to named CSV files and do not overwrite sensitive paths or read local secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are visible CSV examples with fixed relative filenames. I found no evidence of hidden file access, path traversal, or persistence behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static external-command findings point to Markdown code fences, shell installation examples, and API examples. No subprocess, os.system, eval, exec, or shell invocation was found in the Python scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation snippets, not runtime shell execution. Targeted search found no actual process execution APIs in the implementation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto findings align with words such as machine learning examples, sampling terms, citation strings, and documentation text. No hashing, encryption, password storage, or cryptographic verification code was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner matched benign text and parameter names rather than cryptographic APIs. Targeted review found no crypto implementation to evaluate.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTTP GET Requests With User-Supplied Query Parameters
dangerous_patterns.description: The client builds query parameters from user-provided searches and filters, then sends them to OpenAlex. This is expected behavior, but it discloses query content to the API provider.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code explicitly adds parameters and calls requests.get. The destination is fixed to OpenAlex, which limits the concern to expected data disclosure.

dangerous_patterns.title: CSV Export Examples Write Local Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Documentation includes examples that write API results to local CSV files. This can create local artifacts, but the examples use fixed filenames and ordinary research data.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write mode is directly visible in Markdown examples. The context is data export, and I found no evidence of sensitive file targeting.

content.value_statement: Scholarly research queries often require careful OpenAlex filters, pagination, and citation analysis. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical workflows and Python helpers for bibliometric research.
actual_capabilities: Search OpenAlex works by topic, title, abstract terms, year, citation count, and open access status.
Find publications by author or institution using the recommended two-step entity lookup pattern.
Retrieve citation, DOI, ORCID, ROR, journal, topic, and publication metadata from OpenAlex records.
Aggregate publication trends by year, topic, institution, or other supported OpenAlex group fields.
Batch lookup identifiers and paginate large result sets with rate limiting and retry handling.
Export selected research metadata to CSV through documented examples.
limitations: Requires network access to the public OpenAlex API.
OpenAlex coverage and metadata quality vary by field, publisher, and record type.
No API key is required, but rate limits still apply.
Large extractions need careful limits to avoid slow runs and excessive requests.

use_cases.title: Build a Literature Search
use_cases.description: Find recent, highly cited, or open access papers on a defined topic with reproducible OpenAlex filters.

use_cases.title: Analyze Institutional Output
use_cases.description: Summarize publications, citations, open access rates, and topics for a university or research organization.

use_cases.title: Create Bibliometric Datasets
use_cases.description: Collect DOI batches, citation lists, and yearly publication counts for downstream analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Find Papers on a Topic
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the OpenAlex skill to find recent open access papers about climate adaptation. Return titles, years, citations, DOIs, and source names.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Institutions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use OpenAlex to compare MIT and Stanford publications since 2020. Include total works, top topics, citation counts, and open access percentages.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Citations for a Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this DOI to find citing papers through OpenAlex. Summarize recent citing works, publication years, topics, and open access status.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Reproducible Bibliometric Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reproducible OpenAlex query plan for highly cited AI papers from 2022 onward. Include filters, selected fields, pagination limits, and CSV columns.

output_examples.input: Find highly cited CRISPR papers since 2020.
output_examples.output: A ranked paper list with titles, publication years, citation counts, DOIs, source names, and open access status.

output_examples.input: Analyze Stanford University research output from 2020 to 2024.
output_examples.output: A concise report with total works, yearly trends, open access percentage, and the strongest topic areas.

output_examples.input: Prepare a dataset of papers on quantum computing.
output_examples.output: A query plan and export outline with fields such as title, year, citation count, DOI, source, and open access status.

best_practices: Use an email address for the OpenAlex polite pool when running repeated queries.
Resolve author, institution, source, and funder names to OpenAlex IDs before filtering works.
Set result limits and selected fields before running large pagination or export tasks.
anti_patterns: Do not filter by ambiguous human names when OpenAlex IDs are available.
Do not request all pages for broad topics without a maximum result limit.
Do not treat OpenAlex metadata as complete without checking coverage for the research domain.

faq.question: Does this skill require an OpenAlex API key?
faq.answer: No. OpenAlex is open, but the skill recommends an email address for higher polite-pool rate limits.

faq.question: Can it download full paper text?
faq.answer: No. It works with OpenAlex metadata and open access links. Full text access depends on external sources.

faq.question: Can it search by author or institution name?
faq.answer: Yes. It first resolves the name to an OpenAlex ID, then uses that ID for reliable work filters.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: Yes. The Python helpers query the public OpenAlex API and may include your email in request parameters.

faq.question: Can it export results?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes CSV export examples for selected paper metadata.

faq.question: Is it suitable for large bibliometric studies?
faq.answer: Yes, with limits. Use selected fields, pagination caps, batching, and rate limiting for large analyses.
